author: Clement of Rome
interpretation: Clement treats the formation of Adam from dust as demonstrating God's intimate, hands-on creative work. Unlike the spoken commands of Genesis 1, God shapes humanity personally. Clement draws on this to emphasize human dignity and the special care God takes with the church as the body formed by divine hands.
context: Clement does not distinguish two creation accounts; he reads Genesis 1–2 as a continuous narrative. His pastoral use of the material focuses on humility — humanity's origin from dust grounds believers against pride.

author: Irenaeus of Lyon
interpretation: Irenaeus reads Adam's formation as the prototype for incarnation — the same divine hands that shaped Adam from clay would later form Christ in Mary's womb. Adam was created immature, not in a state of full perfection, and was intended to grow into the divine likeness over time. This 'recapitulation' theology sees Christ completing what Adam was meant to become.
context: Irenaeus developed recapitulation theology against Gnostic claims that the material world was evil. By affirming the goodness of Adam's bodily creation, he defended the goodness of matter itself.

author: John Chrysostom
interpretation: Chrysostom insists on the literal formation of Adam from dust and Eve from Adam's rib. He reads the narrative as deliberately anthropomorphic — God condescends to describe divine action in human terms so that ordinary listeners can grasp it. The creation of Eve from Adam's side (not his head or feet) signals equality in dignity and mutual dependence.
context: Chrysostom represents the Antiochene school's commitment to the literal-historical sense. His Genesis homilies were preached to general congregations in Antioch, shaping popular Christian understanding of creation.

author: Thomas Aquinas
interpretation: Aquinas distinguishes the rational soul, which is directly created by God, from the body, which is formed from pre-existing matter. He argues that the formation of Eve from Adam's rib is literally true and theologically necessary — it establishes the unity of the human race from a single origin and signifies the sacramental bond of marriage.
context: Aquinas integrates Aristotelian metaphysics with Genesis: the soul as 'form' of the body maps onto the breath of life animating the dust. His treatment of Eve's creation became standard Catholic teaching on marriage's institution.

author: Martin Luther
interpretation: Luther emphasizes the Garden as a place of vocation — Adam was not idle but was given the work of tending and keeping. Marriage was instituted before the Fall, making it a creation ordinance rather than a concession to sin. Luther reads the naming of the animals as an act of dominion reflecting Adam's original wisdom and righteousness, now lost.
context: Luther's reading elevated the dignity of ordinary labor and marriage against the medieval elevation of celibacy and monastic life. His insistence that work was pre-Fall directly shaped Protestant work ethic theology.

author: John Calvin
interpretation: Calvin sees Genesis 2 as a more detailed account of day six, not a contradictory source. He reads the garden as a bounded sacred space given to Adam as steward, not owner. The command not to eat from the tree of knowledge was a test of obedience — the content of the command was arbitrary, but the principle of submission to God's word was the point.
context: Calvin's harmonization of Genesis 1 and 2 remained the standard Protestant approach until the rise of source criticism. His emphasis on stewardship over ownership influenced Reformed environmental ethics.

author: Julius Wellhausen
source_title: Prolegomena to the History of Israel
interpretation: Wellhausen assigns Genesis 2:4ff to the Yahwist source (J), which he considers the oldest Pentateuchal strand. Unlike the cosmic, liturgical P account in chapter 1, J tells an earthy, anthropomorphic story focused on human experience. The two accounts represent different authors from different periods with different theological concerns, not a single narrative.
context: The distinction between P (Genesis 1) and J (Genesis 2) became a cornerstone of the Documentary Hypothesis. Modern scholars continue to debate the dating and relationship of these sources.

author: John H. Walton
interpretation: Walton reads Genesis 2 as a sequel rather than a contradiction of Genesis 1. Chapter 1 establishes cosmic function; chapter 2 focuses on the archetypal human vocation within sacred space. The garden is a sacred precinct — a proto-temple — and Adam's role of 'working and keeping' uses the same Hebrew terms later applied to priestly service in the tabernacle.
context: Walton's identification of priestly language in Adam's garden vocation has been widely adopted across the theological spectrum, connecting creation to Israel's worship life.
